speaker: interviewer
text: I have Max Veffer. So you worked in Canada as a diamond cutter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I worked as a diamond cutter. My first job I had with the fellas. One's name was Leonard [? Black. ?] The other guy's name was Phil Roth.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Montreal?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was here in Toronto.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Toronto.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Toronto. In the-- the Young Street Arcade. They called themselves the Brilliant-- the Brilliant Diamond Company. They were beautiful. Because-- I came-- I didn't know my way around. Phil Roth picked me up in the morning to work and took me home at night. And I worked for them for about two years. But then their business didn't do so well. So I transferred myself to a place.

speaker: interviewer
text: The family Gross. Gross-- Gross and Company. Gross Diamond Company. They were-- they were on Adelaide and was it Dundas? Yes. Something like that. How long did you work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked there till-- just before I-- before I got married. 1950. That's right. 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nice people. The Gross family were-- they were originally from Belgium. They were nice people. Mark Gross. And he had a sister. How do I know the-- the sister is because every year-- at the-- at the-- because they had a lot of Gentile people working for them-- they had a Christmas party. And she happened to be there. And she was very, very, very nice. And my brother worked there. And that time, while I worked there, I think I got to know my wife. Yeah, I got to know my wife. And I cut the stone for her, my engagement ring. I made it myself. Wasn't big, but it was-- I made it. And I made a ring. We got engaged. And we got married in 1951.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you moved?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1951. Yeah, we-- we-- lived-- I lived for awhile in Montreal. But I says, no. I think I like better Toronto. We moved to Toronto. And because there was no more work, I worked for a while in Montreal as a diamond cutter. And I finally went back to United States. And the Dutch quota was closed. He-- he wanted to take me with him, but the Dutch quota was closed. I couldn't get a visa. So I went back to Toronto. And no diamonds. So the fellow says, well, come on into the flower business. That's the only thing I know about. At that time, they had the store on College Street, where now the store-- [? Labardi. ?] [? Labardi ?] Stores. We had the corner there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was in the business?

speaker: interviewee
text: The business was started with my dad. My dad and my brother Jack. My brother Jack always want to be a doctor, but he became a florist. And that's how it is. My dad always said, you're in the flower business because you were born in between the daffodils and tulips. And that's how he became a-- that's why he had to be a florist. He said, [INAUDIBLE] make a living for my family. I don't mind what I do. But I liked flowers. And from-- from there, we moved up to Wilson Avenue, 1960. 1960, and we were-- we've been there ever since. It was my dad, myself, and my brother Jack, and my brother Joe. And we always made a good living. We made a good living. I-- I bought myself a house. It was cheap in those days. Mind you, I had to borrow the money. I borrowed $2,000, put it down on the house on Bathurst and Steeles. Was cow pasture. We were the first one there. Yeah, was nobody there. We bought a model, paid $2,000 down, which I borrowed at [INAUDIBLE], they had a-- they had a-- an [? auction. ?] They called it an [? auction. ?] You can borrow money at 5%, which was cheap. And I paid that off. House is paid for today, thank God. And my children were born, our three small children. Then my wife got pregnant. And we lost a baby. It was a boy. And she says, I want a boy. Said, well, maybe we'll adopt a boy. And that's how we adopt Jonathan. He was a month-- month, two months old. Beautiful little boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: He grew up. And Dr. Cass, my rabbi says, is he gonna be Jewish? Well, naturally, he's gonna be Jewish. So Dr. Cass-- he was about a year old.

speaker: interviewee
text: But first as a baby, he was circumcised. We gave him a Jewish name. I don't remember exactly what they call him. But they gave him a Jewish name. Dr. Cass, he talked already. Because the reason I say Jonathan talked already-- here's a funny story. We take him to the mikvah, the one on Bathurst Street. It's right near [INAUDIBLE]. I think it's [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: We take him in. I get-- I have to go in with him. He says, Daddy, I have to go there? I say yeah, you have to go in there. OK. So you have to dip him, so that the rabbi can say [NON-ENGLISH]. I put him down. I pick him up. And he's just not very happy with that. We have to put it three times. Put him down again, and he said, Daddy, don't put me down there! You're drowning me!

speaker: interviewee
text: This kid. He says, I have to do one more. You better go with me, otherwise I'm not gonna go down there again. So I had to go with him. I put my head under, and he came down. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: They take-- go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's all right. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we take him to a place because he was circumcised, but they have to-- whatever that was-- we had to take him to the mikvah on Sheppard, and they had to circumcise him again. So we take him there. He says, do I have to do this? Yes, you have to do this. But it will hurt him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they had to do this again. And he's crying bitterly because he knows what's going on. He-- So anyways, the story is, we lived there. Is a fellow around the corner, an old man. He's about 85. He always walks my dogs. [? I have-- ?] always in my life-- we must have had 10 dogs.

speaker: interviewee
text: We still have three now. But we almost-- my family likes dogs. Either my daughter likes dogs, and then they don't keep them. They give them to us. So this fellow comes around and Jonathan is playing outside.

speaker: interviewee
text: And his face-- my daughter-- my Jonathan comes walking with him around the corner. So as she comes out, she says-- this is after he went to the-- to circumcise-- the circumcisement. And he says to Dave, Dave. He says, yeah? He says, don't let them cut your putzer. Said, why? He says, that hurts! He says-- and I laughed. Because I'm standing-- I'm listening to this. I said, Dave-- Dave says, what is he telling me? So I have to tell him the whole story. He says, I can believe that that hurts. You know? So-- we laughed. It was a very funny story. My Jonathan is a good boy. Tell me the names of your daughters.

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest one is Sharon. And Sharon, her husband is-- he works for TTC. And then I have Marla. And her husband works for Simpson, [? Sears. ?] . And then I have Barbie. And her husband works for ADT. And Jonathan is not married. Jonathon lives in Berry. He's got himself a girlfriend there. And I have good kids. I'm blessed with good girls, yeah, like I said.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just mentioned to me that your father had passed away 25 years ago. But I wanted you to go back over a story that-- when your father gave you a nickel for helping him--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I used to do funny things for those nickels. My godfather used to come and visit me all the time. And I would take a walk with him. My dad gave me a nickel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had known he liked cigars. So one day my dad gives me [INAUDIBLE] and says, come on. Said, where are we going? Said, come on! Follow me. I walk into the cigar store. I says, give me a nice cigar for a nickel for my grandfather. And my grandfather, you're buying me a cigar? Said, why? Said, because I love you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Go back, he says, Jonas, your daughter-- your son bought me a cigar, here. There's a coupon too. So my dad gave me another nickel. He said, that was nice. I'll do that again. These are good stories. They're-- I like to remember the good stories. The bad ones you can have, you like to forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me when you think about what enabled you to survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mental. Mentally equipped.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: Self-discipline. You-- you discipline yourself to think good things not bad things. Always think good things. If they're bad, you cancel them out. You were to just cancel them. Something bad comes into your mind, cancel. And that's how mentally equipped and self-discipline. And but-- to do that, you have to feel good about yourself. I always said to myself, might be a good kid. And to good people come good things. So don't worry about it. You're gonna get out of here. You're gonna get out of here. Sooner or later, you're gonna get out of here. And if you keep on doing it to yourself-- even today in business, I use to do the same thing. I would-- somebody would walk in and would want to work for me. If I don't like you, you haven't got a chance. I-- I surround myself with good people. I told you that before. I supply the people that I can love, so they can love me back. I can repeat it forever. I like the people that hug me so I can hug them back. And I like the people that kiss. And I kiss them back. And that means the people that do that, they're good people. And my kids are the same way. They phone my wife every day. Every day. When they get up in the morning, and when I go to sleep. I can get phone calls.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like, I jump out of my bed, 11 o'clock at night, 11:30. Who is that? Sharon. Why? I just want to talk to you. Goodnight. I love you. I love you back. Those are the simple words. And that's how you get to-- that's how you get through what we went through.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's simple as that. Life is very simple. If you live a simple life, you'd be surprised. You see, there's two things. I heard the other day too, and I think the same way. Take every day and find out five good things every day. You don't have to write them down. If you're forgetful, write them down. And you go at the end of the week, and see how many good things happened to you. You'd be surprised how good you feel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, do you think that something like the Holocaust could happen again?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? Because there's no Hitler around. If Hitler would have let the Jewish people alone, he would have-- he would have owned the world. I'm a firm believer in that. If he would have left the Jewish people alone, he would have been able to concentrate on the-- on the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Conquer the world, so to speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: One last question that I'd like to ask. What would you hope people would learn, perhaps your grandchildren, great grandchildren, from watching this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like them to believe or that it was-- take it as an education. Take it as a case-- as case and then believe and be sure that they understand-- I think I said it before-- how we suffered and the pains, the ordeals and the pain that we endured. And doing that, by watching this, if they-- if they have a bad memory, write it down and read it over and over again if they forget. Because they should not forget. They should fight for a free, free world. Peaceful-- a peaceful world. So they can enjoy what we enjoyed when we kids-- were kids, before the war. Let it be like it was before the war. Let it be horse and buggy. Let it be anything, but let it be like it was before the war. And if they believe in that, I think my grandchildren and my children will have a better world. And I'm sure that-- I like to believe that. And I wish it to them, because they should not go through what we went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi, I like to show you. This picture is me in the middle, at age two, and my brother on the right, [? and ?] [? so ?] my brother Jacob, and my sister Chellie on the left side. I think that's-- it's like an antique.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, presumably that was taken in Amsterdam, because I was two years old. And I live-- I lived there till I was four years old. This is my grandfather on my mother's side. I remember-- remember him very well because of the wholesale vegetable place that he had in Bussum.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name-- my namesake, Meyer. Meyer Nebig. And this is my grandmother on my mother's side. This is, of course, the wife of my grandfather that I just showed you before. She was a very nice lady. I used to go have lunch at her place when I went to school there. It was only about five minutes from school. I remember her very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Rachel, Rachel Nebig.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Since you asked me, it was [? Waterman. ?] Oh, this is a good picture. This was fun time. We were-- we used to go in the summertime to Zandvoort. And we stayed there for the summertime. On the right side is my mother, may she rest in peace. And in the-- the background of her on the right hand side is my brother Alan. And below him is myself, Mey-Pie. And then below him is Juda. And beside him in the middle there is my little brother Johnny. And above him is my grandfather, the father on my father's side. Great man. I loved him. My father was similar to him. He always had a cigar. This is the fellow that I bought a cigar for a nickel for.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name? Jonas, namesake of my father. And below him is-- there's my grandmother on my-- on my father's side. And her name, it was Clara. Her maiden name escapes me. I'm sorry. And then beside her is my sister Chellie. We had a lot of fun in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, this-- this picture brings fond memories. That's little Mey-Pie at the age of about six years old. And see the station that I told you about, that I climb up? That's the station I climbed up on top and brought my soccer ball back. This is-- make me things fond memories. Those were, again, the good days.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my beloved dad. I miss him very much. I used to think of him as a-- my role model. And he was a simple person. Give him a cigar in his mouth, and he was a happy man. I'm just sad that I couldn't say goodbye to him. He passed away in Los Angeles on holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, his name was Jonas, Jonas Veffer. And this is my dear mom, may she rest in peace. Very strong person. She got us through this war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Sarah, Sarah Nebig. If it wasn't for her, I wouldn't be-- be talking to you right now. The little doll that sits next her is the local dress-- the way they dress in Volendam. It's like where you heard from the wooden shoes, those are the people with the wooden shoes. Well, this is a picture that shows you how we were treated during the war. This was in-- in '39. And the local black shirts, we used to call them, the facists-- that was the flower shop. They-- they-- they painted this on and made sure that everybody know that we're Jewish. But we didn't care. We did a good day's business.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: They just overnight-- we got up in the morning, we saw this painted on our-- on our window. And they wouldn't let us take this off. Somebody, black shirts, painted it on. They thought it was important.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then you told me that the people of the town all came to buy that day.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. We had a good day's business. I like to forget about this. Well, we spent in that house, a thousand days. That was the room that you see above on the top. We spent 1,000 days with eight people in that house. And you see the window on the side. That's the window that I sat for hours and hours, and looking at the people that were coming by. And when we had the raid, I was the one that tipped everybody off that we had a raid, that we had to hide in the-- in the-- underneath the chair-- the stairs.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the address?

speaker: interviewee
text: The address, it was 28 [NON-ENGLISH]. And this picture, this was taken when I took a trip to Europe, and we wound up in Amsterdam. And I went to walk with my wife to the old Jewish ghetto, so to speak. It was sad because I walked in the street where all my aunties and-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Where all my aunties and my cousins used to live. And there was just-- their faces were not there anymore. That was the only bad day I had on that trip. And I was glad to see it again because it brought some good memories. But to see it again, it just was not the same as it used to be. The whole district was not the Jewish people that-- that used to live there. And this is best to be forgotten because there's nothing I can change about that. This is taken on the-- on the same trip over to Holland. It's the synagogue that the inside where I had my bar mitzvah, and my sister got married to my brother-in-law. And that's fond memories because they told me-- I wish there was video-- that I davened pretty good. And everything and the family got together. And I think that was a fun day. Yeah, this was a real fun day. That was the day my sister got married to Martin Stern. You can see me on the left, top, in my top hat. And like I said, it was a blessed day for my sister because she's still married to my brother-in-law, and they have five nice children. There's my mom on the right hand side. And then on the left hand-- bottom side, the left, is my uncle Louie and my Auntie Rose. That's my-- sister of my father. She's still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was April 4, 1946.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have to agree with you, if that's what's marked down. I can't-- I can't actually not remember. But since that is what you said, that's what it was. And it was a fun day. Yes, here is Auntie Fifi, Mrs. [INAUDIBLE]. We also went to visit on our holidays. If it wasn't for that lady-- and she is a lady, or if she's gone, I don't know. I haven't spoken to her lately. She saved our lives. She brought our food to keep us alive. And we're-- we really are indebted to her because there were not many people like that who would do what she did during the war. This is a memorial in Amsterdam they put up for people that passed away during the war in the concentration camps. This is a picture taken in Zandvoort. We had a little bit of fun. This is my friend Sydney Drucker with his back to us, and my friend Simon [? Wertheim. ?] We used to be a bit of amateur boxers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're-- This is me in the middle, and then Simon is on the right. This is my first week in Toronto. They used to take pictures on Young Street. That's, of course, me on the right, and my sister Chellie. And we walked-- we took a stroll on Young Street. Well, this goes back 46 years ago. I got married to my wife, Sylvia. And I-- every year I buy her some poems, and this is the one that's special to me. 'Being with you is the insurance I need to make me realize that you are not only part of my dreams but have become the most necessary part of my life. And I sincerely mean that.' And here's my dear dad and my mom at my wedding. And that was a-- a wonderful day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names are?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jonas and Sarah. Well, here's a picture. We were fortunate enough to be on a Ralph Edwards show, And the life story was of my mother's book. And it was a wonderful day. We were there for three days. And there is from left to right is Johnny, the top line is Jack, Chellie, myself, Juda, Jack. And then from left-- on the left side is Auntie Fifi, my mom, Sarah, and my father Jonas. And of course, there's Ralph Edwards, and his helper, the assistants on the right hand side. It was a wonderful day. And here is a-- a picture of my mom, very surprised, that the lady that kept us in hiding, Mrs. Makkinje-- my mother was very surprised that they brought her over for the show. It was a wonderful thing that they did for her. These are the days on Wilson Avenue in my flower shop. And like again, I enjoyed my work there. And this is in front of the flower shop. And my good friend Simon came to visit me after I visit Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. This was, oh, I'd say about 15 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your friend Simon [? Wertheim ?] is on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's on the left, and I'm in the middle, and my wife, Sylvia on the right. And this is a picture taken a while back. That is my daughter Sharon, on the left, and my daughter Marla in the middle, and my daughter Barbie on the right, and myself on the left, and Jonathan in the middle, and my dear wife on the right. And it was taken a while back. They are beautiful daughters. I'm blessed with a beautiful daughter and beautiful wife and beautiful son. And this is one of my best days in my life. This is my adopted son, Jonathan. And he's growing up to be a-- a beautiful man. He's now 27 years old. This was an occasion of my Marla's wedding on the right hand side. My Jonathan, you can see how tall he is. He's now 27 years old. And my wife. And on the left is my daughter Barbara. You can see we're animal lovers. There's Sparky on the bottom and Brandy on the top. And they're the love of my wife's life. And another pair of my wife's friends. There's Spunky in the front and Crystal little chicken there [LAUGHS] in-- in the back. They're beautiful dogs. Well, here's a pleasant picture of myself and Sylvia. On the right is Dana. In the middle is Lee. Lee is an expert-- Judo expert. He's got a-- a-- a green belt. And he won already many trophies. And there's a little one is my little baby. And she is Lindzay. And she's the pride and joy of our life.
